facts_disclosed: ProMotion Sports Clinic was acquired by PhysioFirst Group
Dr. Faisal left after the acquisition
New management is corporate-focused on KPIs and patient volume

turns.content: Ugh I need to vent. I've been holding this in for weeks and I'm about to explode haha. Things at my clinic have gone completely sideways.
I'm here for it — vent away. What's going on at the clinic?

So ProMotion got bought out. This corporate group called PhysioFirst Group acquired us about two months ago. They came in with all the usual promises — 'we love your culture, nothing will change, we're just providing resources.' Classic, right? And then EVERYTHING changed.

That's a really common pattern with acquisitions, unfortunately. The initial reassurances rarely hold up once the new management starts implementing their systems. What kind of changes are you seeing on the ground?

Everything is about numbers now! Patient volume targets, KPIs for every little thing, session length limits. They want me to see more patients in less time, which means less quality time with each person. I became a PT to HELP people, not to run them through like a factory line, querida.

The tension between patient-centered care and volume-based metrics is one of the biggest issues in healthcare right now. Are they actually measuring clinical outcomes, or is it purely throughput numbers?

Purely throughput! That's what kills me! They don't care that my patients get better faster because I spend more time with them. They just see 'Bruna saw six patients today, the target is eight.' Ai meu Deus, it makes me want to scream. And the worst part — the absolute worst — Dr. Faisal left. He's gone.
Losing a leader like that during an acquisition can really shake the foundation of a whole team. Was his departure directly related to the PhysioFirst takeover?

Completely! He told me directly — he said he refused to compromise the way he practices medicine for some corporate board. And honestly I respect him SO much for that, but it left us all kind of orphaned. He was the one who trusted us, who let me build my rehab programs my way. Now I have a regional manager who doesn't know the difference between a rotator cuff and a cuff link telling me my average session length is too long. Nossa!

That's incredibly frustrating — having clinical decisions second-guessed by someone without clinical expertise is one of the most demoralizing things in healthcare. Do you have any allies in the new management structure, or is it entirely non-clinical people making the calls?

There's supposed to be a clinical director being hired but who knows when that will happen. Right now it's all business people. And look, I understand a clinic needs to make money — I'm not naive about that. But there's a difference between being sustainable and being a patient mill. Some of my colleagues are just going along with it. The younger ones think this is how clinics work — KPIs, volume targets, everything measured and optimized.

The generational divide makes sense — if you've only known corporate healthcare, it seems normal. But the ones who worked under Dr. Faisal's model know what's possible when clinical leadership drives the culture. How are you holding up personally?

Honestly? It's affecting me. I used to LOVE going to work, I would practically run through the door. Now I sit in my car for five minutes just mentally preparing myself. And the saudade for how things used to be is real, gente. I miss walking in and Dr. Faisal being there with his coffee saying 'Bruna, what miracle are we doing today?' That man understood what this work is about.

That shift from excitement to dread is a real warning sign for burnout, and you're right to pay attention to it. Have you thought about what your options look like — whether that's advocating for changes within PhysioFirst, or exploring something else entirely?

Every day haha. But it's complicated — my visa is tied to them, starting a clinic in Dubai is insanely expensive, the licensing is intense. My mae keeps saying 'Bruna, come home, open a clinic in Sao Paulo' and honestly some days the saudade is so strong that sounds really good. But no, I think Dubai is still where I need to be. The opportunities here are incredible, I just need the RIGHT environment.

The visa constraint is real and it limits your ability to just walk away. But you're right that Dubai still has opportunities — the sports rehab market there is growing fast. In the meantime, have you considered documenting your patient outcomes? If you can show that your longer sessions lead to better results, that's data-driven language that corporate management actually responds to.

That's actually a really smart approach! Fight the corporate machine with corporate weapons haha. I do have better patient satisfaction scores than anyone on the team, and my return-to-sport timelines are consistently ahead of the benchmarks. Maybe I need to put that into a presentation format they can understand. Show them that quality care IS good business.

Exactly — frame it in terms of outcomes, patient retention, and referral rates. If your patients come back and recommend others because of the quality of care, that's direct revenue. Corporate management might not understand clinical excellence, but they understand numbers that support the bottom line.

You know what, you're right. I think I was so busy being angry that I forgot I can actually make a case for doing things my way. I'm going to start pulling my outcome data this weekend. My mother says if I survived moving across the world alone, I can survive some corporate nonsense. She's right!

Your mother sounds wise. And your instinct to stay and fight for the quality of care you believe in says a lot about you as a clinician. Build that outcomes case, keep treating your patients the way they deserve, and come back to let me know how it goes.
I will! Thank you meu bem, I really needed this conversation. Let PhysioFirst try to argue with RESULTS! My patients are not numbers and I'm going to prove it, querida!
That's the spirit. Good luck with the data — you've got this.
